questions.question: How does the final grade for the Research Workshop break down between the two assessed products?

options.A: 60% group paper and 40% individual exam, both averaged together
options.B: 40% group paper and 60% individual exam, and both must pass
options.C: 50% group paper and 50% individual exam, with no pass requirement
options.D: 40% individual exam and 60% group paper, and both must pass

questions.explanation: The paper is worth 40% and the exam 60%, and both the group product (paper) and the individual product (exam) must pass independently. A inverts the weighting; C invents a 50/50 split with no pass rule; D swaps which product carries which weight.

questions.question: A student plans to use the GenAI tools they relied on for analysis only in the final paper and skip the Technology Statement. Is this approach RIGHT or WRONG, and why?

options.A: RIGHT — the Technology Statement is optional and unnecessary once the tools are cited somewhere in the paper body
options.B: RIGHT — disclosure is only required if GenAI wrote the actual paper text
options.C: WRONG — the Technology Statement is a separate required declaration of any GenAI or tooling used
options.D: WRONG — GenAI use is banned, so there is nothing to declare in any statement

questions.explanation: The Technology Statement is a mandatory, separate declaration of any GenAI or tooling used anywhere in the project, not a citation in the paper. A and B wrongly treat it as optional; D is wrong because GenAI is not banned, its use simply must be disclosed.

questions.question: Which document is required to be completed BEFORE any data collection may begin?

options.C: The final paper following the example structure
options.D: Ethical Standards Compliance with the informed consent template

questions.explanation: Ethics compliance plus the informed consent template must be in place before data collection starts. The schedule template (A) and the Team Expectations Agreement (B) are planning artefacts, not data-collection gates; the final paper (C) comes after data and analysis.

questions.question: On Canvas a student is placed into an auto-assigned group. To work on a supervisor's research topic, what must they do?

options.A: Self-enroll in the research-topic group, since auto-assigned groups are not the topic groups
options.B: Stay in the auto-assigned group, which is already the correct research-topic group
options.C: Email the supervisor and ask to be manually moved out of every Canvas group that was auto-created
options.D: Wait for the supervisor to merge the auto group into the topic group

questions.explanation: Canvas auto-assigned groups are NOT the research-topic groups, so students must self-enroll around a supervisor's topic. B confuses the two group types; C and D invent procedures that are not how enrollment works.

questions.question: A team proposes a study with 1 condition and 15 participants, analysed in Excel. Is this design RIGHT or WRONG against the minimum project requirements?

options.A: RIGHT — a single rich condition with 15 well-characterised participants comfortably exceeds the stated minimum
options.B: RIGHT — the analysis tool is free choice as long as participants are enough
options.C: WRONG — it needs at least 2 conditions, at least 20 participants per condition, and R or Python
options.D: WRONG — only because Excel is banned; 1 condition and 15 participants are fine

questions.explanation: The minimum is at least 2 conditions, at least 20 participants per condition, and analysis in R or Python; this design fails all three. A and B ignore the condition count and tooling rules; D fixes only the tool while leaving the condition and sample-size violations unaddressed.

questions.question: Which sequence correctly lists the plenary lecture topics in the order the course presents them?

options.A: Experimental design, RQ and literature review, writing, then data analysis
options.B: Data analysis, writing, RQ and literature review, then experimental design
options.C: Writing, data analysis, experimental design, then RQ and literature review
options.D: RQ and literature review, experimental design, data analysis, then writing

questions.explanation: The plenary lectures move from RQ and literature review, to experimental design, to data analysis, and finally to writing. A, C, and D shuffle these stages out of the logical research progression that the course follows.

questions.question: Which statement best captures the purpose of the Team Expectations Agreement (TEP)?

options.A: It is the ethics form authorising the team to collect data from participants
options.B: It is a written contract on roles, deadlines, and conflict resolution within the team
options.C: It is the spreadsheet used to plan the project milestones and timeline
options.D: It is the statement disclosing which GenAI tools the team relied on

questions.explanation: The TEP is a written contract covering team roles, deadlines, and how conflicts are resolved, which helps manage issues like free-riders. A is the ethics document, C is the Project Schedule Template, and D is the Technology Statement.

questions.question: What is the stated primary exam material that the exam is built from?

options.A: The plenary lecture slides, which cover all the plenary lectures
options.B: The HS and ML example proposals that are posted on Canvas for student reference
options.C: The Belcher book chapter on writing the literature review
options.D: The group's own final paper and its analysis results

questions.explanation: The exam covers all plenary lectures, and the slides are the primary exam material. The example proposals (B) are structural templates, Belcher (C) is a recommended reference, and the paper (D) is the separately graded group product, not exam source material.

questions.question: A student on the Machine-Learning track expects the same example proposal as a Human-Studies peer. Is that expectation RIGHT or WRONG?

options.A: RIGHT — a single shared example proposal serves both tracks equally
options.B: RIGHT — track choice only affects the supervisor, not the example materials
options.C: WRONG — the HS and ML tracks have different example proposals on Canvas
options.D: WRONG — example proposals exist only for the Human-Studies track

questions.explanation: The HS and ML tracks have distinct example proposals reflecting their different method focus. A and B wrongly assume one shared template; D wrongly claims ML students have no example proposal at all.

questions.question: Which option best states an intended learning outcome of the course?

options.A: Formulate testable research questions, design valid studies, run analyses, and write and defend a paper
options.B: Memorise the slide deck so the exam can be passed without any supervision sessions
options.C: Publish a peer-reviewed journal article in a top-tier venue before the semester ends
options.D: Complete a literature review only, leaving the design and the analysis to the supervisor

questions.explanation: The learning outcomes are to formulate testable RQs, design valid studies, run analyses, and write and defend a paper, practising the full research cycle. A reduces the course to rote recall, C overstates the deliverable as a publication, and D omits the core design and analysis work students must do.
